seoTitle: Espressif Products | ESP32 & ESP8266 Wi-Fi SoCs | BeiLuo
seoDescription: Explore Espressif comprehensive product portfolio including ESP32 Wi-Fi SoCs, Bluetooth modules, combo modules, and development kits. Authorized distributor with technical support.

faqs.question: What are the main product categories offered by Espressif?
faqs.answer: Espressif offers four core product categories: Wi-Fi SoCs featuring the popular ESP8266 and ESP32 series with integrated wireless connectivity and powerful processors, Bluetooth SoCs including low-power BLE solutions and Bluetooth mesh capable devices, Combo Modules providing pre-certified Wi-Fi and Bluetooth combinations with integrated antennas for rapid deployment, and Development Boards offering comprehensive evaluation platforms with rich peripheral support for prototyping IoT applications. Each category is designed to provide complete wireless connectivity solutions with minimal external components.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse our product categories to find the right Espressif components for your IoT application. Contact our FAE team for personalized product recommendations.

faqs.question: How do I select the right Espressif product for my IoT application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right Espressif product involves analyzing your connectivity requirements, processing needs, power budget, and form factor constraints. For Wi-Fi-only applications with cost sensitivity, ESP8266 remains an excellent choice. For applications requiring both Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, ESP32 provides the ideal solution. For battery-operated BLE devices, consider ESP32-C3 or ESP32-C6. For AI-enabled applications, ESP32-S3 offers vector instructions for machine learning. For rapid prototyping and production, pre-certified modules like ESP-WROOM-32 accelerate time-to-market. Our technical support team can provide detailed comparisons based on your specific design parameters.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our product selection guides or contact our FAE engineers for expert assistance in choosing the optimal Espressif components.

faqs.question: What support does BeiLuo provide for Espressif products?
faqs.answer: As an authorized Espressif distributor, BeiLuo provides comprehensive support including technical consultation for wireless design and antenna optimization, product selection assistance based on application requirements, reference design review for RF performance, ESP-IDF framework and Arduino support, programming and firmware flashing services, supply chain management and inventory planning, competitive pricing and volume discounts, fast delivery from local stock. Our FAE team has deep expertise in Espressif ecosystem and IoT application development.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our technical support team for immediate assistance with your Espressif product inquiries and design challenges.

faqs.question: Which Espressif products are best suited for smart home applications?
faqs.answer: Espressif products are ideal for smart home applications requiring reliable wireless connectivity. The ESP32-WROOM-32E module provides dual-band Wi-Fi and Bluetooth for smart speakers and hubs. ESP32-C3 offers cost-effective Wi-Fi and BLE5 for smart lighting and switches. ESP32-S3 provides AI acceleration for voice recognition and image processing in security cameras. ESP8266 remains suitable for simple sensor nodes and smart plugs. All Espressif products support popular smart home protocols including MQTT, HTTP, and can integrate with Alexa, Google Home, and Home Assistant platforms.
faqs.decisionGuide: Filter products by smart home application requirements or contact our IoT FAE team for recommendations on optimal solutions.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ESP32, ESP32-S2, ESP32-S3, and ESP32-C3?
faqs.answer: ESP32 is the original dual-core Wi-Fi + Bluetooth SoC with Xtensa LX6 processors. ESP32-S2 is a single-core Wi-Fi only variant with lower cost and USB OTG support. ESP32-S3 adds AI vector instructions, improved security features, and USB OTG while maintaining dual-core performance. ESP32-C3 features a single-core RISC-V processor with Wi-Fi and BLE5, offering better power efficiency at lower cost. ESP32-C6 adds Wi-Fi 6 and Thread/Zigbee support for smart home applications. Choose based on your connectivity needs, processing requirements, and cost constraints.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose ESP32 for general Wi-Fi+BT applications, ESP32-S3 for AI/ML, ESP32-C3 for cost-sensitive BLE, ESP32-C6 for Wi-Fi 6 and Matter.

categories.name: Wi-Fi SoCs
categories.description: Espressif Wi-Fi SoCs combine wireless connectivity with powerful embedded processors, offering complete IoT solutions in single chips with minimal external components.
categories.longDescription: As an authorized Espressif distributor, BeiLuo provides comprehensive selection and support for Espressif's Wi-Fi SoC portfolio. The ESP8266 revolutionized IoT with its cost-effective Wi-Fi connectivity, while the ESP32 series added Bluetooth, more processing power, and advanced peripherals. Whether you need simple Wi-Fi connectivity for sensor nodes or powerful processing for IoT gateways, our FAE team can guide you through the selection process. We stock popular chips including ESP8266EX, ESP32-D0WD, and ESP32-S3 with local inventory and technical support. Our selection guide helps you choose the right Espressif SoC based on your connectivity, processing, and power requirements.

selectionGuide.title: How to Select Espressif Wi-Fi SoCs
selectionGuide.description: Comprehensive guide for choosing the right Wi-Fi SoC for your IoT application

faqs.question: How do I choose between ESP8266 and ESP32?
faqs.answer: Choose ESP8266 for cost-sensitive Wi-Fi-only applications with basic processing needs. It offers the lowest cost Wi-Fi connectivity with sufficient performance for simple sensor nodes and smart plugs. Choose ESP32 when you need Bluetooth connectivity, more processing power, additional peripherals, or advanced security features. ESP32's dual-core architecture provides 7.5x more processing power than ESP8266, making it suitable for complex IoT applications requiring local processing and edge computing capabilities.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ESP8266 for simple Wi-Fi applications under $2 BOM cost. Use ESP32 for anything requiring Bluetooth or significant processing.

faqs.question: What Wi-Fi standards do Espressif SoCs support?
faqs.answer: ESP8266 supports 802.11 b/g/n in 2.4GHz band with maximum 72.2 Mbps PHY rate. ESP32 adds 802.11 b/g/n with both station and soft-AP modes simultaneously, supporting up to 150 Mbps. ESP32-C6 introduces 802.11 ax (Wi-Fi 6) support with improved efficiency and range. All Espressif Wi-Fi SoCs support common security protocols including WPA/WPA2/WPA3, WEP, and WAPI. The Wi-Fi stack is fully integrated with TCP/IP offload, requiring minimal host processor intervention for wireless communication.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ESP8266/ESP32 for standard Wi-Fi 4 applications. Use ESP32-C6 for Wi-Fi 6 and improved power efficiency.

faqs.question: How do I estimate power consumption for battery applications?
faqs.answer: Espressif SoCs offer multiple power modes for battery optimization: Active mode consumes 120-240mA depending on processor load and Wi-Fi activity. Modem-sleep mode reduces consumption to 20-25mA while maintaining Wi-Fi connection. Light-sleep mode achieves 0.8-2mA with GPIO wakeup capability. Deep-sleep mode reaches 5-10uA with RTC memory retention, ideal for long battery life. Use the ULP coprocessor in ESP32 for sensor monitoring at 100uA. Typical battery-powered sensors using deep-sleep can achieve 1-5 year battery life on AA batteries with proper duty cycling.
faqs.decisionGuide: Implement aggressive sleep modes with periodic wake-up for battery applications. Use ESP32-C3 for best power efficiency.

faqs.question: What development frameworks are available for Espressif SoCs?
faqs.answer: Espressif provides multiple development options: ESP-IDF is the official C/C++ framework with FreeRTOS, offering full hardware access and professional development features. Arduino IDE support enables rapid prototyping using familiar Arduino libraries and syntax. MicroPython and CircuitPython provide Python scripting for quick development and education. PlatformIO offers cross-platform development with advanced debugging. ESPHome simplifies smart home device development with YAML configuration. NodeMCU Lua firmware enables Lua scripting for rapid IoT development. All frameworks are actively maintained with extensive community support.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ESP-IDF for production applications. Use Arduino for rapid prototyping. Use MicroPython for education and scripting.

faqs.question: How do I select the right package for my application?
faqs.answer: Espressif SoCs are available in multiple packages: QFN48 (5x5mm) for ESP8266 with minimal external components. QFN48 (6x6mm) for ESP32 with more GPIO options. ESP32-WROOM modules integrate SoC, flash, crystal, and antenna in compact 18x25mm modules with FCC/CE certification. ESP32-WROVER adds PSRAM for applications requiring more RAM. ESP32-MINI modules offer smaller 15x18mm footprint. For space-constrained designs, ESP32-PICO-D4 integrates all components in 7x7mm SIP package. Module selection simplifies certification and reduces design complexity compared to chip-down designs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use pre-certified modules for faster time-to-market. Use chip-down designs for high-volume cost optimization.

products.name: ESP32 Wi-Fi & Bluetooth Module
products.shortDescription: Popular ESP32 module with dual-core processor, Wi-Fi and Bluetooth connectivity, 4MB flash, and FCC/CE certification for IoT applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The ESP32-WROOM-32E is Espressif's most popular Wi-Fi and Bluetooth module featuring the ESP32-D0WD-V3 dual-core SoC.
With integrated 4MB flash, PCB antenna, and complete RF calibration, it provides a ready-to-use wireless solution for IoT devices.
The module is FCC, CE, IC, TELEC, and SRRC certified, eliminating the need for additional wireless certification in most applications.

faeReview.author: Michael Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Wireless Connectivity
faeReview.content: The ESP32-WROOM-32E has been my go-to recommendation for IoT projects requiring both Wi-Fi and Bluetooth. In my 8 years supporting wireless designs, I've seen this module deployed in everything from smart home devices to industrial sensors. The pre-certification saves months of regulatory testing and thousands of dollars in certification costs. The dual-core architecture allows for simultaneous Wi-Fi stack processing and application code execution without performance degradation. I particularly appreciate the extensive GPIO availability and rich peripheral set including CAN, Ethernet MAC, and touch sensors. For developers new to Espressif, the Arduino IDE support makes getting started incredibly easy, while ESP-IDF provides professional-grade tools for production firmware. The module's reliability has been proven in millions of deployed devices worldwide.

alternativeParts.reason: Adds 8MB PSRAM for applications requiring more RAM for graphics or data buffering
alternativeParts.useCase: Applications requiring additional RAM for complex applications or camera interfaces

alternativeParts.reason: Lower cost alternative for Wi-Fi-only applications without Bluetooth requirements
alternativeParts.useCase: Cost-sensitive Wi-Fi-only applications like smart plugs and basic sensors

companionParts.description: Official development board for ESP32 evaluation and prototyping
JTAG debugger and programmer for ESP32 development
3.3V LDO for ESP32 power supply
External crystal for accurate clock generation

faqs.question: What is the maximum data rate of ESP32-WROOM-32E?
faqs.answer: The ESP32-WROOM-32E supports maximum Wi-Fi PHY data rates of 150 Mbps in 802.11n mode with 40MHz channel width. In practical TCP/IP throughput testing, typical sustained data rates are 30-50 Mbps depending on environmental conditions and protocol overhead. The module supports both 20MHz and 40MHz channel bandwidths, with 40MHz providing higher throughput but potentially more interference in crowded 2.4GHz environments. For most IoT applications requiring periodic data transmission, the available bandwidth is more than sufficient.
faqs.decisionGuide: For higher throughput requirements, ensure optimal antenna placement and consider 40MHz channel width.

faqs.question: How much flash memory does ESP32-WROOM-32E have?
faqs.answer: The ESP32-WROOM-32E includes 4MB (32Mbit) of integrated SPI flash memory for program storage and data logging. This is sufficient for most IoT applications including OTA (Over-The-Air) firmware updates. The flash is organized with a partition table that can be customized to allocate space between firmware, file systems (SPIFFS or LittleFS), and OTA update storage. For applications requiring more storage, ESP32-WROVER modules with external PSRAM are available, or external SPI flash can be added to chip-down designs.
faqs.decisionGuide: 4MB is sufficient for most applications. Use ESP32-WROVER for applications requiring additional RAM.

faqs.question: What Bluetooth profiles are supported by ESP32-WROOM-32E?
faqs.answer: ESP32-WROOM-32E supports Bluetooth 4.2 including both Classic Bluetooth and Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE). Classic Bluetooth supports profiles including SPP, A2DP, AVRCP, and HFP for audio and serial applications. BLE supports GAP, GATT, SMP, and L2CAP protocols with peripheral and central modes. BLE mesh networking is supported for large-scale device networks. The Bluetooth stack is integrated with the ESP-IDF framework, providing APIs for custom profile development. Coexistence with Wi-Fi is managed internally for reliable dual-mode operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Classic Bluetooth for audio applications. Use BLE for low-power sensor devices and mesh networks.

faqs.question: What is the range of ESP32-WROOM-32E Wi-Fi?
faqs.answer: The ESP32-WROOM-32E Wi-Fi range depends on environmental conditions and antenna configuration. With the integrated PCB antenna, typical indoor range is 30-50 meters through walls, and outdoor line-of-sight range can exceed 100 meters. The module supports transmit power up to +20dBm, which can be adjusted to balance range and power consumption. For extended range applications, external antennas can be connected to modules with antenna connectors. The 802.11n standard with MIMO support provides better range and reliability compared to older 802.11g devices.
faqs.decisionGuide: For extended range, use external antennas and ensure clear line-of-sight where possible.

faqs.question: What is the power consumption in different modes?
faqs.answer: ESP32-WROOM-32E power consumption varies by operating mode: Active mode with Wi-Fi transmitting consumes 120-250mA depending on TX power. Active mode with CPU running but Wi-Fi idle consumes 80-120mA. Modem-sleep mode (Wi-Fi connected, radio off between beacons) consumes 20-25mA. Light-sleep mode (CPU paused, GPIO wakeup) consumes 0.8-2mA. Deep-sleep mode (RTC only, timer wakeup) consumes 5-10uA. The ULP coprocessor can run at 100uA for sensor monitoring. Proper power management can achieve years of battery life for sensor applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use deep-sleep with periodic wake-up for battery-powered sensors. Use modem-sleep for always-connected devices.

faqs.question: Is ESP32-WROOM-32E certified for regulatory compliance?
faqs.answer: Yes, ESP32-WROOM-32E is fully certified for major regulatory regions including FCC (USA), CE (Europe), IC (Canada), TELEC (Japan), and SRRC (China). These certifications cover both Wi-Fi and Bluetooth radio operation. The module certification allows integration into end products without additional RF testing or certification in most cases, significantly reducing time-to-market and certification costs. Always verify specific certification coverage for your target markets and consult with regulatory experts for products requiring medical, automotive, or safety certifications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use certified modules to avoid costly and time-consuming regulatory testing for your end product.

products.name: ESP32-C3 Wi-Fi & BLE5 Module
products.shortDescription: Cost-effective ESP32-C3 module with RISC-V processor, Wi-Fi and BLE5 connectivity, ideal for battery-powered IoT devices.
descriptionParagraphs: The ESP32-C3-WROOM-02 is a cost-effective Wi-Fi and BLE5 module featuring the ESP32-C3 single-core RISC-V SoC.
With improved power efficiency and Bluetooth 5.0 support, it is ideal for battery-operated IoT devices and smart home applications.
The module maintains FCC/CE certification while offering a more economical alternative to the dual-core ESP32.

faeReview.author: Sarah Johnson
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Low-Power Wireless
faeReview.content: The ESP32-C3-WROOM-02 is my top recommendation for cost-sensitive IoT applications requiring both Wi-Fi and Bluetooth. The RISC-V architecture provides excellent power efficiency compared to Xtensa cores, making it ideal for battery-powered devices. I've successfully deployed this module in smart home sensors achieving over 2 years battery life on coin cells. The BLE5 support with mesh networking capability is a game-changer for large-scale device deployments. The module form factor is slightly smaller than ESP32-WROOM, helping with compact designs. For developers concerned about RISC-V compatibility, Espressif has done an excellent job maintaining API compatibility with ESP32, making migration straightforward. The price point makes it competitive with BLE-only modules while providing full Wi-Fi capability.
faeReview.highlight: Best cost-performance for Wi-Fi + BLE5 applications

alternativeParts.reason: Higher performance dual-core option with Classic Bluetooth support
alternativeParts.useCase: Applications requiring more processing power or Classic Bluetooth audio

alternativeParts.reason: Adds Wi-Fi 6 and Thread/Zigbee support for smart home applications
alternativeParts.useCase: Smart home devices requiring Matter protocol support

companionParts.description: Compact development board for ESP32-C3 evaluation
Coin cell holder for battery-powered prototypes
Low-quiescent LDO for battery applications
RTC crystal for accurate sleep timing

faqs.question: What is the difference between ESP32-C3 and ESP32?
faqs.answer: ESP32-C3 uses a single-core RISC-V processor instead of the dual-core Xtensa LX6 in ESP32. While raw processing power is lower, the RISC-V core offers better power efficiency and lower cost. ESP32-C3 supports BLE 5.0 compared to BLE 4.2 in ESP32, providing longer range and higher speed for Bluetooth applications. ESP32-C3 does not support Classic Bluetooth, only BLE. Wi-Fi performance is similar with 802.11n support. ESP32-C3 is ideal for cost-sensitive and battery-powered applications, while ESP32 remains better for processing-intensive tasks.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose ESP32-C3 for BLE5 and cost-sensitive battery applications. Choose ESP32 for higher processing power and Classic Bluetooth.

faqs.question: How power-efficient is ESP32-C3 compared to ESP32?
faqs.answer: ESP32-C3 offers approximately 20-30% better power efficiency than ESP32 due to the RISC-V architecture and improved process technology. In active mode, ESP32-C3 consumes around 80-100mA compared to 120-150mA for ESP32. Deep-sleep current is similar at 5-10uA. The improved efficiency translates to longer battery life for portable devices. For a typical sensor application waking every 10 minutes to send data, ESP32-C3 can achieve 30-40% longer battery life compared to ESP32 using the same battery capacity.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ESP32-C3 for battery-powered applications where every milliamp counts.

faqs.question: Does ESP32-C3 support Bluetooth mesh?
faqs.answer: Yes, ESP32-C3 supports Bluetooth 5.0 including Bluetooth mesh networking. Bluetooth mesh enables large-scale device networks with up to 32,000 nodes, making it ideal for smart home and commercial lighting applications. The mesh protocol allows devices to relay messages through intermediate nodes, extending network coverage beyond single-hop Bluetooth range. ESP32-C3 can operate as a mesh node, relay, or provisioner. The ESP-IDF framework includes a complete Bluetooth mesh stack with sample applications for lighting and sensor networks.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ESP32-C3 for Bluetooth mesh applications requiring Wi-Fi gateway connectivity.

faqs.question: What is the RISC-V processor in ESP32-C3?
faqs.answer: ESP32-C3 features a single-core 32-bit RISC-V RV32IMC processor running at up to 160MHz. The RV32IMC designation indicates support for the base integer instruction set (I), multiplication and division (M), and compressed instructions (C). This provides good performance for IoT applications while maintaining low power consumption. The RISC-V architecture is open-source, providing long-term availability and avoiding proprietary licensing. Espressif provides full GCC toolchain support and the ESP-IDF framework is optimized for the RISC-V core.
faqs.decisionGuide: RISC-V offers open architecture benefits. Code migration from ESP32 Xtensa is straightforward with ESP-IDF.

faqs.question: Can I use Arduino IDE with ESP32-C3?
faqs.answer: Yes, ESP32-C3 is fully supported in the Arduino IDE through the ESP32 board package. Install the ESP32 Arduino core version 2.0.0 or later to get ESP32-C3 support. Most Arduino libraries compatible with ESP32 work on ESP32-C3 with minimal or no modifications. The Arduino framework abstracts the underlying RISC-V architecture, so existing ESP32 Arduino code typically compiles and runs without changes. Some low-level register access code may need modification due to architectural differences between Xtensa and RISC-V.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Arduino IDE for rapid prototyping. Use ESP-IDF for production applications requiring full hardware access.

faqs.question: What antenna options are available for ESP32-C3-WROOM-02?
faqs.answer: ESP32-C3-WROOM-02 features an integrated PCB antenna optimized for 2.4GHz Wi-Fi and Bluetooth operation. The antenna provides good performance with typical gain of 2-3dBi. For applications requiring external antennas, the ESP32-C3-WROOM-02U variant includes a U.FL connector for external antenna connection. Using an external antenna can improve range by 3-6dB depending on antenna selection. The module includes antenna diversity support for improved reliability in challenging RF environments. Always follow Espressif's layout guidelines for optimal antenna performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use standard module for compact designs. Use -02U variant with external antenna for extended range requirements.

categories.name: Bluetooth SoCs
categories.description: Low-power Bluetooth SoCs optimized for BLE applications, supporting Bluetooth 5.0, mesh networking, and extended range for battery-operated IoT devices.
categories.longDescription: Espressif's Bluetooth SoC portfolio delivers industry-leading power efficiency for BLE applications. The ESP32-C2 and ESP32-C6 series combine Bluetooth 5.0 with Wi-Fi connectivity, enabling versatile IoT designs. These SoCs feature ultra-low power consumption in sleep modes, making them ideal for battery-powered sensors, beacons, and wearable devices. With support for Bluetooth mesh networking, they can scale to thousands of nodes in commercial and industrial deployments. BeiLuo provides comprehensive support for Bluetooth SoC selection, including power profiling, antenna design, and firmware development assistance.

selectionGuide.title: Bluetooth SoC Selection Guide
selectionGuide.description: Guide for selecting the right Bluetooth SoC for low-power applications

faqs.question: What Bluetooth versions do Espressif SoCs support?
faqs.answer: Espressif Bluetooth SoCs support Bluetooth 5.0 and 5.3 standards. Key features include 2x speed (2 Mbps PHY), 4x range (coded PHY with 125/500 kbps), and 8x broadcasting capacity compared to Bluetooth 4.2. Bluetooth mesh networking is fully supported for large-scale device deployments. ESP32-C6 adds support for 802.15.4 (Thread/Zigbee) in addition to Bluetooth, enabling Matter protocol compliance for smart home applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ESP32-C3/C6 for Bluetooth 5.0 with Wi-Fi. Use ESP32-H2 for dedicated Bluetooth/802.15.4 applications.

faqs.question: How do I optimize power consumption for BLE applications?
faqs.answer: Optimize BLE power by using connection intervals of 100ms or longer, leveraging slave latency to skip connection events, transmitting at 0dBm or lower when range permits, using deep sleep between advertising/connection events, and disabling unnecessary peripherals. ESP32-C3 achieves 5uA in deep sleep with RTC timer wakeup. With proper optimization, BLE sensors can achieve 1-3 years on coin cell batteries with typical 1-second advertising intervals.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use long connection intervals and aggressive sleep modes. Consider energy harvesting for perpetual operation.

faqs.question: What is Bluetooth mesh and when should I use it?
faqs.answer: Bluetooth mesh is a many-to-many communication protocol enabling thousands of devices to communicate in a network. Messages can relay through intermediate nodes, extending network coverage beyond single-hop Bluetooth range. Ideal applications include commercial lighting (up to 32,000 nodes), building automation, and sensor networks. ESP32-C3 and ESP32-C6 support all Bluetooth mesh node types including relay, proxy, friend, and low-power nodes. The ESP-IDF provides a complete mesh stack with provisioning and security features.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Bluetooth mesh for networks exceeding 20 devices or requiring multi-hop communication.

faqs.question: Can Espressif SoCs support Matter protocol?
faqs.answer: Yes, ESP32-C6 and ESP32-H2 support the Matter protocol through integrated 802.15.4 (Thread) and Bluetooth Low Energy. Matter is the new universal smart home standard backed by Apple, Google, Amazon, and others. Espressif provides Matter SDK and pre-certified solutions, enabling devices to work seamlessly across ecosystems. The dual-protocol support allows commissioning over BLE and operation over Thread/Wi-Fi networks. BeiLuo can provide Matter development kits and certification support.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ESP32-C6 or ESP32-H2 for Matter-compatible smart home devices.

faqs.question: What is the typical range of BLE on Espressif SoCs?
faqs.answer: BLE range depends on PHY mode and environment: 1 Mbps PHY provides 30-50m indoor range. 500 kbps coded PHY extends range to 100-150m. 125 kbps coded PHY achieves 200-300m range with reduced data rate. External antennas can further extend range. The ESP32-C3 with integrated PCB antenna achieves typical 50m line-of-sight range. For extended range applications, ESP32-C3-WROOM-02U with external antenna connector is recommended.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use coded PHY for extended range. Use external antennas for maximum coverage.

products.name: ESP32-C6 Wi-Fi 6 & BLE5 Module
products.shortDescription: Advanced ESP32-C6 module with Wi-Fi 6, Bluetooth 5.0, and 802.15.4 support for next-gen IoT and Matter applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The ESP32-C6-WROOM-01 is Espressif's first module with Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax) support, featuring the ESP32-C6 RISC-V SoC.
With integrated Bluetooth 5.0 and 802.15.4 (Thread/Zigbee), it enables Matter protocol compliance for universal smart home connectivity.
Wi-Fi 6 features like OFDMA and Target Wake Time significantly improve power efficiency and network capacity in dense deployments.

faeReview.author: David Liu
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Emerging Technologies
faeReview.content: The ESP32-C6-WROOM-01 represents the future of IoT connectivity with Wi-Fi 6 and Matter support. I've been working with early adopters on Matter-compatible smart home products, and the feedback has been overwhelmingly positive. The Wi-Fi 6 features like OFDMA dramatically improve performance in congested environments - we've seen 40% better throughput in apartment building deployments compared to Wi-Fi 4. The integrated 802.15.4 radio enables Thread connectivity without additional chips, simplifying Matter device design. Power consumption is excellent with Target Wake Time enabling sub-mA average current for battery sensors. For developers building next-generation smart home products, this module provides future-proof connectivity that works across all major ecosystems.
faeReview.highlight: Future-proof with Wi-Fi 6 and Matter support

alternativeParts.reason: Lower cost alternative without Wi-Fi 6 and Thread requirements
alternativeParts.useCase: Standard IoT applications not requiring Matter or Wi-Fi 6

alternativeParts.reason: Dedicated Bluetooth/Thread solution without Wi-Fi for ultra-low-power designs
alternativeParts.useCase: Pure Thread/Bluetooth mesh devices like sensors and switches

companionParts.description: Development board for ESP32-C6 with Wi-Fi 6 evaluation
Module programmer for production flashing
High-PSRR LDO for RF applications
External 2.4GHz antenna for extended range

faqs.question: What are the benefits of Wi-Fi 6 in ESP32-C6?
faqs.answer: Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax) in ESP32-C6 provides several advantages: OFDMA enables efficient multi-device communication in dense networks. Target Wake Time (TWT) allows devices to schedule wake periods, reducing power consumption by up to 60%. BSS Coloring improves performance in congested environments with multiple networks. 20MHz-only operation is optimized for IoT devices. In real-world testing, ESP32-C6 maintains stable connections with 100+ devices on a single access point, where Wi-Fi 4 would struggle.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ESP32-C6 for dense IoT deployments or when Matter compatibility is required.

faqs.question: How does Matter protocol work on ESP32-C6?
faqs.answer: ESP32-C6 supports Matter through dual-radio operation: Bluetooth LE is used for device commissioning (pairing) and initial setup. Thread (802.15.4) provides the primary communication protocol for the Matter mesh network. Wi-Fi 6 enables high-bandwidth communication and cloud connectivity. This allows ESP32-C6 devices to work seamlessly across Apple HomeKit, Google Home, Amazon Alexa, and other Matter-compatible ecosystems. Espressif's Matter SDK simplifies development with pre-certified examples for common device types.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ESP32-C6 for universal smart home devices that work across all major platforms.

faqs.question: What is 802.15.4 and why is it important?
faqs.answer: 802.15.4 is the IEEE standard for low-rate wireless personal area networks, forming the foundation of Thread and Zigbee protocols. It operates in the 2.4GHz band with 250 kbps data rate, optimized for low-power, low-cost device communication. Unlike Wi-Fi, 802.15.4 uses mesh networking where devices relay messages, extending network range and reliability. ESP32-C6's integrated 802.15.4 radio eliminates the need for separate Thread/Zigbee chips, reducing BOM cost and complexity for Matter devices.
faqs.decisionGuide: 802.15.4 is essential for Matter and Thread applications. ESP32-C6 integrates this capability.

faqs.question: Can ESP32-C6 replace separate Wi-Fi and Thread chips?
faqs.answer: Yes, ESP32-C6 can replace separate Wi-Fi and Thread chips in most IoT applications. The integrated dual-radio solution reduces BOM cost by $2-4 compared to discrete chip implementations. PCB area is reduced by approximately 50mm². Software complexity is simplified with unified ESP-IDF development. However, applications requiring simultaneous high-throughput Wi-Fi and Thread operation may benefit from dedicated radios. For typical smart home sensors, switches, and controllers, ESP32-C6 provides an optimal integrated solution.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ESP32-C6 for integrated Wi-Fi + Thread designs. Use discrete chips only for specialized high-performance applications.

faqs.question: What development tools are available for ESP32-C6?
faqs.answer: ESP32-C6 is supported by Espressif's complete development ecosystem: ESP-IDF v5.1+ with full hardware abstraction layer. Arduino ESP32 core v3.0+ for familiar development. Matter SDK for universal smart home applications. ESP-ADF for audio applications. VS Code extension with debugging support. ESP-Prog and ESP-Module-Prog for flashing. Comprehensive API reference and application examples. BeiLuo provides development kits and technical support to accelerate your ESP32-C6 projects.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ESP-IDF for production Matter devices. Use Arduino for rapid prototyping.

faqs.question: How do I migrate from ESP32 to ESP32-C6?
faqs.answer: Migrating from ESP32 to ESP32-C6 requires several considerations: Update ESP-IDF to version 5.1 or later. Adapt to RISC-V architecture (most ESP-IDF code is architecture-agnostic). Review peripheral differences - some ESP32 peripherals are not available on ESP32-C6. Update partition tables for 4MB flash. Consider Wi-Fi 6 features for power optimization. Test Thread/Matter functionality if applicable. Espressif provides migration guides and code examples. BeiLuo FAEs can assist with migration planning and troubleshooting.
faqs.decisionGuide: Plan migration early in development. Leverage Espressif migration guides and BeiLuo support.

products.name: ESP32-H2 Bluetooth 5.3 & 802.15.4 Module
products.shortDescription: Ultra-low-power ESP32-H2 module dedicated to Bluetooth 5.3 and 802.15.4 for Thread/Zigbee applications without Wi-Fi.
descriptionParagraphs: The ESP32-H2-MINI-1 is Espressif's first SoC without Wi-Fi, optimized purely for Bluetooth 5.3 and 802.15.4 protocols.
With an ultra-low-power RISC-V processor and optimized radio design, it achieves industry-leading power efficiency for battery-operated devices.
The module is ideal for Thread end devices, Zigbee sensors, and Bluetooth mesh nodes requiring multi-year battery life.

faeReview.author: Emily Zhang
faeReview.title: FAE - Low-Power Wireless
faeReview.content: The ESP32-H2-MINI-1 is a game-changer for battery-powered Thread and Zigbee devices. By removing Wi-Fi and optimizing for 802.15.4, Espressif has created the most power-efficient module in their lineup. I've measured deep sleep currents below 2uA with RTC retention - that's coin cell battery territory. The Bluetooth 5.3 support includes the latest features like LE Audio and enhanced attribute protocol. For Matter end devices like sensors and switches that don't need Wi-Fi, this module is perfect. The compact MINI footprint (15x16.5mm) fits in the smallest devices. Pricing is very competitive against dedicated Zigbee chips from other vendors. If you're building battery-powered smart home devices, the H2 should be at the top of your list.
faeReview.highlight: Ultra-low power for battery-operated Thread/Zigbee devices

alternativeParts.reason: Adds Wi-Fi 6 capability for devices requiring cloud connectivity
alternativeParts.useCase: Thread border routers or devices needing both Thread and Wi-Fi
TI ecosystem customers or specific Zigbee applications

companionParts.description: Compact dev board for ESP32-H2 evaluation
600mAh coin cell for long-life prototypes
Ultra-low-Iq buck converter for battery apps
Compact chip antenna for space-constrained designs

faqs.question: Why would I choose ESP32-H2 over ESP32-C6?
faqs.answer: Choose ESP32-H2 when your application doesn't need Wi-Fi and prioritizes ultra-low power consumption. The H2 achieves 1.5uA deep sleep current versus 5uA for C6. For Thread end devices like sensors and switches that communicate through a border router, H2 provides optimal battery life. Choose ESP32-C6 when you need Wi-Fi for direct cloud connectivity or as a Thread border router. The H2 is also more cost-effective for high-volume deployments of simple Thread/Zigbee devices.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use H2 for pure Thread/Zigbee end nodes. Use C6 when Wi-Fi is required or for border routers.

faqs.question: What is the battery life with ESP32-H2?
faqs.answer: Battery life with ESP32-H2 depends on application requirements. A typical temperature sensor sending data every 10 minutes can achieve 3-5 years on a CR2450 coin cell. A door/window sensor with event-driven reporting can last 2-3 years on a CR2032. Bluetooth beacons advertising every second can operate 1-2 years on the same battery. The key is aggressive use of deep sleep (1.5uA) and minimizing active time. With energy harvesting from solar or vibration, perpetual operation is possible for many applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Implement aggressive sleep modes and event-driven reporting for maximum battery life.

faqs.question: Does ESP32-H2 support Matter?
faqs.answer: Yes, ESP32-H2 fully supports Matter as an end device. It can join Matter networks over Thread (802.15.4) and be commissioned via Bluetooth LE. This enables H2-based devices to work with Apple HomeKit, Google Home, Amazon Alexa, and other Matter-compatible platforms. As a Thread end device, it communicates through Thread border routers (which can be ESP32-C6 or other platforms) to reach the IP network. Espressif's Matter SDK includes pre-certified examples for common device types like sensors, switches, and locks.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use H2 for Matter-compatible battery-powered end devices like sensors and switches.

faqs.question: What is Bluetooth 5.3 and what's new?
faqs.answer: Bluetooth 5.3 is the latest core specification with several enhancements: LE Audio support for audio streaming over BLE. Enhanced Attribute Protocol (EATT) for improved concurrent operations. Connection Subrating for faster connection parameter updates. Channel Classification Enhancement for better coexistence with Wi-Fi. Periodic Advertising Enhancement for more efficient broadcasting. ESP32-H2 is among the first SoCs to support Bluetooth 5.3, future-proofing your designs for emerging BLE applications including audio and advanced wearables.
faqs.decisionGuide: Bluetooth 5.3 provides latest features and future-proofs your BLE designs.

faqs.question: How do I develop with ESP32-H2?
faqs.answer: ESP32-H2 development uses the same ESP-IDF framework as other Espressif chips: Install ESP-IDF v5.1 or later with H2 support. Use ESP32-H2-DevKitM-1 for evaluation and prototyping. Develop with standard C/C++ using ESP-IDF APIs. Flash via USB using the onboard USB-to-UART bridge. Debug with ESP-Prog JTAG debugger. Access Thread and Zigbee stacks through ESP-IDF components. Use ESP-Matter SDK for Matter applications. BeiLuo provides development kits and can assist with Thread network setup and certification.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ESP-IDF for production development. Use DevKitM-1 for evaluation and prototyping.

faqs.question: Can ESP32-H2 work with existing Zigbee networks?
faqs.answer: Yes, ESP32-H2 supports Zigbee 3.0 and can join existing Zigbee networks as an end device or router. The 802.15.4 radio is compatible with Zigbee Pro and Zigbee Home Automation profiles. Espressif provides a Zigbee SDK with examples for common device types like lights, switches, and sensors. Note that Zigbee and Thread share the 802.15.4 radio but use different network stacks - ESP32-H2 can support either protocol but not simultaneously. For new designs, Thread/Matter is recommended for better interoperability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use H2 for Zigbee if integrating with existing Zigbee ecosystems. Prefer Thread/Matter for new designs.

categories.name: Combo Modules
categories.description: Pre-certified Wi-Fi and Bluetooth combo modules with integrated antennas, flash, and crystals for rapid IoT product development.
categories.longDescription: Espressif combo modules integrate SoC, flash memory, crystal, and antenna in compact, pre-certified packages. These modules eliminate the complexity of RF design and regulatory certification, allowing you to focus on application development. With FCC, CE, IC, TELEC, and SRRC certifications included, you can bring products to market months faster than chip-down designs. BeiLuo stocks popular modules including ESP-WROOM-32, ESP-WROVER, and ESP32-MINI series with local inventory and technical support for antenna integration and regulatory compliance.

selectionGuide.title: Combo Module Selection Guide
selectionGuide.description: Guide for selecting pre-certified modules based on size, features, and certification requirements

faqs.question: What are the benefits of using pre-certified modules?
faqs.answer: Pre-certified modules offer significant advantages: Eliminate 3-6 months of RF certification time for FCC, CE, IC, and other regions. Save $10,000-50,000 in certification testing costs. Avoid complex RF design and antenna tuning. Reduce PCB design complexity and risk. Ensure consistent RF performance across production. Simplify supply chain with single part number. Faster time-to-market for IoT products. The module certification typically covers your end product, requiring only minimal additional testing for intentional radiator compliance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use pre-certified modules for faster time-to-market. Use chip-down designs only for very high volumes.

faqs.question: How do I choose between different module sizes?
faqs.answer: Module size selection depends on your product constraints: ESP-WROOM-32 (18x25.5mm) - Standard size with full features. ESP-WROVER (18x31.4mm) - Larger with PSRAM for memory-intensive apps. ESP32-MINI-1 (15x18mm) - Compact for space-constrained designs. ESP32-PICO-D4 (7x7mm) - Ultra-compact SIP with all components integrated. Consider PCB space, enclosure size, antenna requirements, and thermal management. Smaller modules may have limited GPIO or antenna options.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose the smallest module that meets your GPIO and antenna requirements. Consider future expansion needs.

faqs.question: What certifications do Espressif modules include?
faqs.answer: Espressif modules typically include: FCC (USA) Part 15 certification for intentional radiators. CE (Europe) RED directive compliance. IC (Canada) RSS certification. TELEC (Japan) certification. SRRC (China) certification. KCC (Korea) certification. NCC (Taiwan) certification. These certifications cover the module's RF emissions and immunity. When integrating into your product, you may need additional testing for unintentional radiator compliance and product safety (UL, IEC), but the wireless certification burden is significantly reduced.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify specific certifications for your target markets. Contact BeiLuo for certification documentation.

faqs.question: Can I use external antennas with Espressif modules?
faqs.answer: Yes, many Espressif modules support external antennas: ESP32-WROOM-32U includes U.FL connector for external antenna. ESP32-WROVER-IB has IPEX connector and antenna diversity. ESP32-C3-WROOM-02U variant supports external antennas. Using external antennas can improve range by 3-6dB and allows antenna placement away from interference sources. However, using an external antenna not included in the original module certification may require additional testing. Always consult Espressif's antenna design guide for optimal performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use U/W variants with external antennas for extended range or when module must be shielded.

faqs.question: What is the difference between WROOM and WROVER modules?
faqs.answer: WROOM modules include SoC, flash, crystal, and PCB antenna in a compact package. WROVER modules add external PSRAM (Pseudo-Static RAM) for applications requiring more memory. WROVER modules are slightly larger (18x31.4mm vs 18x25.5mm) and include an IPEX antenna connector. Choose WROOM for standard IoT applications with 4MB flash. Choose WROVER when you need additional RAM for graphics, camera buffering, or complex data processing. Both offer the same Wi-Fi and Bluetooth capabilities.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use WROOM for most applications. Use WROVER when additional RAM is required for your application.

products.name: ESP32 WROVER Module with PSRAM
products.shortDescription: Enhanced ESP32 module with 8MB PSRAM, 4MB flash, and IPEX antenna connector for memory-intensive IoT applications.
descriptionParagraphs: The ESP32-WROVER-E is an enhanced version of the popular ESP32-WROOM module featuring 8MB of external PSRAM.
With the IPEX antenna connector and antenna diversity support, it provides improved RF performance and flexibility.
The additional PSRAM enables complex applications like camera interfaces, graphics displays, and large data buffering.

faeReview.author: James Wilson
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Embedded Systems
faeReview.content: The ESP32-WROVER-E is my recommendation for any ESP32 application that pushes memory boundaries. The 8MB PSRAM is a game-changer for camera projects - I've used it with OV2640 cameras to capture and process VGA images entirely on the ESP32. The IPEX connector allows placing the antenna at the optimal location in the enclosure, improving range by 20-30% compared to PCB antennas in metal enclosures. Antenna diversity provides reliable connections in challenging RF environments. For voice applications, the extra RAM enables buffering for wake-word detection and audio processing. If your application involves cameras, displays, or large data sets, the WROVER is worth the slightly higher cost and larger size.
faeReview.highlight: 8MB PSRAM enables camera and graphics applications

alternativeParts.reason: Lower cost alternative without PSRAM requirements
alternativeParts.useCase: Standard IoT applications not requiring additional RAM

alternativeParts.reason: Upgraded with AI acceleration and more flash

companionParts.description: Full-featured dev kit with LCD and camera interface
External 2.4GHz antenna for WROVER
2MP camera module for ESP32 camera projects
2.4-inch TFT LCD for ESP32 graphics

faqs.question: What can I do with 8MB PSRAM on ESP32-WROVER?
faqs.answer: The 8MB PSRAM on ESP32-WROVER enables applications that exceed the internal 520KB SRAM: Camera interfaces buffering full VGA frames (300KB+). Graphics displays with frame buffers for smooth UI. Large data logging buffers for batch uploads. Audio processing with long sample buffers. Machine learning models with larger weights. TLS/SSL with larger certificate buffers. Web servers serving larger files. The PSRAM is accessed via SPI at 40MHz, providing adequate bandwidth for most applications while freeing internal SRAM for stack and heap.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use WROVER when your application needs more than 300KB RAM for data buffers or graphics.

faqs.question: How do I use the IPEX antenna connector?
faqs.answer: The IPEX (U.FL) connector on ESP32-WROVER-E allows connecting external antennas: Use IPEX to SMA adapter cables for testing with standard antennas. Select 2.4GHz antennas with 50Ω impedance. Place antenna away from metal enclosures and interference sources. Keep antenna cable under 10cm to minimize losses. Consider antenna diversity for improved reliability. The external antenna can provide 3-6dB gain over PCB antennas, significantly extending range. Always follow Espressif's layout guidelines for ground planes and keep-out areas around the antenna connection.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use external antennas when the module is inside metal enclosures or when maximum range is required.

faqs.question: What is antenna diversity and how does it work?
faqs.answer: Antenna diversity uses two antennas to improve wireless reliability. The ESP32-WROVER-E supports antenna diversity through the IPEX connector and an external antenna switch. The system automatically selects the antenna with better signal quality, reducing dead spots caused by multipath fading. This is especially valuable in mobile applications or environments with reflective surfaces. Diversity can improve connection stability by 10-20dB in challenging RF environments. Implementation requires an external RF switch and control from ESP32 GPIO.
faqs.decisionGuide: Implement antenna diversity for mobile applications or challenging RF environments.

faqs.question: Can I use WROVER for camera applications?
faqs.answer: Yes, ESP32-WROVER-E is excellent for camera applications. The 8MB PSRAM can buffer full VGA frames (640x480) at 16-bit color depth. ESP32's dual-core processor can handle JPEG compression at 15-30fps. The ESP-WHO framework provides face detection and recognition examples. Common camera modules like OV2640 and OV7670 are supported. Typical applications include security cameras, QR code scanners, and machine vision for IoT. For higher resolution requirements, consider ESP32-S3 with its AI acceleration capabilities.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use WROVER for VGA camera applications. Use ESP32-S3 for higher resolution or AI-enhanced vision.

faqs.question: How do I access PSRAM in my code?
faqs.answer: Accessing PSRAM in ESP-IDF is straightforward: Enable PSRAM support in menuconfig. Include <esp_heap_caps.h> header. Use heap_caps_malloc(size, MALLOC_CAP_SPIRAM) for PSRAM allocation. Or enable CONFIG_SPIRAM_USE_MALLOC to include PSRAM in standard malloc. PSRAM is mapped to the address space above 4MB flash. Access speed is slower than internal SRAM (40MHz SPI vs 240MHz internal), so keep frequently accessed data in internal RAM. The ESP-IDF memory management automatically handles PSRAM initialization at boot.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use PSRAM for large buffers and data structures. Keep stack and critical data in internal SRAM.

faqs.question: What are the power consumption implications of PSRAM?
faqs.answer: PSRAM adds some power consumption: Active access consumes ~20-30mA additional current. PSRAM can be powered down in deep sleep to save power. Enable CONFIG_SPIRAM_MEMTEST to verify PSRAM at boot. For battery applications, minimize PSRAM access frequency. The benefits of PSRAM usually outweigh the power cost for applications that would otherwise need external storage or complex streaming. With proper power management, WROVER can achieve similar battery life to WROOM for intermittent data acquisition applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Power down PSRAM in deep sleep. Minimize PSRAM accesses for battery-powered designs.

products.name: ESP32 Compact MINI Module
products.shortDescription: Ultra-compact ESP32 module in 15x18mm form factor with integrated PCB antenna for space-constrained IoT designs.
descriptionParagraphs: The ESP32-MINI-1 is Espressif's compact module solution, reducing footprint by 40% compared to standard WROOM modules.
Despite the smaller size, it maintains full ESP32 functionality with dual-core processor, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and 4MB flash.
The module is ideal for wearable devices, compact sensors, and IoT products with strict size constraints.

faeReview.author: Lisa Chen
faeReview.title: FAE - Consumer Electronics
faeReview.content: The ESP32-MINI-1 is a lifesaver for wearable and compact IoT designs. At 15x18mm, it fits where WROOM modules simply won't. I've used it in smart jewelry projects, compact fitness trackers, and even a smart pet collar. Despite the smaller size, you still get the full dual-core ESP32 with all peripherals - no compromises on functionality. The 24 available GPIOs are sufficient for most compact designs. RF performance is comparable to WROOM thanks to the optimized PCB antenna design. For ultra-compact designs where every millimeter counts, the MINI-1 is the obvious choice. Just be aware that there's no PSRAM option and the antenna cannot be external.
faeReview.highlight: 40% smaller than WROOM with full ESP32 features

alternativeParts.reason: Larger module with more GPIO and external antenna option
alternativeParts.useCase: Applications not constrained by size requiring more GPIO

alternativeParts.reason: Even smaller SIP package with crystal and flash integrated
alternativeParts.useCase: Extreme space constraints requiring chip-scale package

companionParts.description: Compact dev board with ESP32-MINI-1
Ultra-low-power accelerometer for wearables
Heart rate and SpO2 sensor for health wearables
Compact lithium polymer batteries for wearables

faqs.question: How much smaller is ESP32-MINI-1 compared to WROOM?
faqs.answer: ESP32-MINI-1 measures 15x18mm (270mm²) compared to ESP32-WROOM-32E at 18x25.5mm (459mm²). This represents a 41% reduction in PCB area. The height is also reduced from 3.1mm to 2.4mm. The smaller size makes MINI-1 ideal for wearables, compact sensors, and IoT devices with strict size constraints. Despite the smaller footprint, MINI-1 maintains full ESP32 functionality including dual-core processor, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and 4MB flash.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use MINI-1 when PCB space is critical. Use WROOM for designs with more space and GPIO requirements.

faqs.question: What GPIOs are available on ESP32-MINI-1?
faqs.answer: ESP32-MINI-1 provides 24 GPIO pins compared to 38 on WROOM modules. The available GPIOs include: SPI, I2C, UART, PWM, and ADC functionality. GPIOs for SDIO, Ethernet MAC, and some specialized functions may be limited or unavailable compared to WROOM. For most IoT applications requiring sensors, actuators, and basic connectivity, 24 GPIOs are sufficient. Check the ESP32-MINI-1 datasheet for the specific GPIO mapping to ensure it meets your peripheral requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Verify GPIO requirements against the MINI-1 datasheet before selecting for your design.

faqs.question: Is RF performance affected by the smaller size?
faqs.answer: ESP32-MINI-1 maintains comparable RF performance to WROOM modules despite the smaller size. The PCB antenna is optimized for the compact form factor and provides similar gain (2-3dBi). Range and sensitivity specifications are equivalent to WROOM. The smaller size may have slightly different radiation patterns, but overall link budget remains comparable. For most applications, users won't notice any performance difference. FCC and CE certifications confirm compliance with regulatory requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: RF performance is comparable to WROOM. Choose based on size requirements without RF concerns.

faqs.question: Can I use external antennas with ESP32-MINI-1?
faqs.answer: No, ESP32-MINI-1 only supports the integrated PCB antenna. There is no U.FL connector or pad for external antenna connection. If your application requires an external antenna for range extension or placement flexibility, consider ESP32-WROOM-32U or ESP32-WROVER-IB modules which include IPEX connectors. The MINI-1 is designed for compact designs where the integrated antenna is sufficient and external antenna routing would be impractical.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use MINI-1 for integrated antenna designs. Use WROOM-U or WROVER-IB for external antenna requirements.

faqs.question: What are typical wearable applications for ESP32-MINI-1?
faqs.answer: ESP32-MINI-1 is ideal for wearable applications: Smart watches and fitness bands with heart rate monitoring. Smart jewelry with activity tracking and notifications. Pet trackers with GPS and activity monitoring. Smart glasses with audio and sensor integration. Medical wearables for continuous health monitoring. The compact size fits within wearable form factors while providing full Wi-Fi and Bluetooth connectivity. Low-power modes enable 1-3 day battery life with typical usage patterns.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use MINI-1 for any wearable requiring wireless connectivity in a compact form factor.

faqs.question: How do I program ESP32-MINI-1?
faqs.answer: ESP32-MINI-1 is programmed the same way as other ESP32 modules: Use ESP32-DevKitM-1 for development and initial programming. Connect UART TX/RX pins to USB-to-UART bridge for flashing. Hold BOOT button (GPIO0 low) while resetting to enter download mode. Use ESP-IDF, Arduino IDE, or PlatformIO for development. Flash via serial at 921600 baud or faster. For production, use ESP-Module-Prog or custom programming fixtures. The programming process is identical to WROOM modules.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use DevKitM-1 for development. Use ESP-Module-Prog for production programming.

categories.name: Development Boards
categories.description: Official Espressif development boards and evaluation kits with comprehensive peripheral support for rapid IoT prototyping.
categories.longDescription: Espressif development boards provide everything needed to start developing with ESP32 and ESP8266. These boards include USB-to-UART bridges, voltage regulators, reset/flash buttons, and expansion headers for easy prototyping. Official DevKit boards from Espressif ensure compatibility and reliability, while third-party boards offer various form factors and features. BeiLuo stocks official DevKitC, DevKitM, and WROVER-KIT boards with local availability and technical support for getting started with ESP32 development.

selectionGuide.title: Development Board Selection Guide
selectionGuide.description: Guide for choosing the right development board for your ESP32 projects

faqs.question: Which development board should I start with?
faqs.answer: For beginners, ESP32-DevKitC is the recommended starting point. It features the popular ESP32-WROOM-32 module, USB-to-UART bridge for easy programming, reset and boot buttons, and breadboard-compatible headers. The DevKitC works seamlessly with Arduino IDE and ESP-IDF. For ESP32-C3 projects, use ESP32-C3-DevKitM-1. For camera applications, ESP32-CAM or ESP32-WROVER-KIT are ideal. All official DevKit boards include voltage regulators and protection circuits for safe development.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with DevKitC for general ESP32 development. Choose variant-specific kits for specialized chips.

faqs.question: What is included on Espressif DevKit boards?
faqs.answer: Official Espressif DevKit boards include: ESP32 module (WROOM, MINI, or C3 depending on variant). USB-to-UART bridge (CP2102 or CH340) for programming and serial output. 5V to 3.3V voltage regulator (AMS1117 or similar). EN (reset) and BOOT buttons for entering download mode. LED indicators for power and GPIO. Pin headers breaking out all available GPIOs. Some kits include additional features like LCD displays, camera connectors, or SD card slots. The boards are designed for breadboard use with standard 0.1-inch spacing.
faqs.decisionGuide: DevKit boards include everything needed for development - just connect USB and start coding.

faqs.question: Can I use Arduino IDE with Espressif DevKit boards?
faqs.answer: Yes, all Espressif DevKit boards work with Arduino IDE. Install the ESP32 Arduino core through Boards Manager (add ESP32 package URL). Select your board variant from Tools > Board menu. Choose the correct COM port for the USB-to-UART bridge. DevKitC users should select 'ESP32 Dev Module'. The Arduino framework abstracts hardware differences, so code written for DevKitC works on other ESP32 boards with minimal changes. Arduino provides a gentle learning curve for ESP32 development with thousands of compatible libraries.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Arduino IDE for rapid prototyping and learning. Use ESP-IDF for advanced features and production.

faqs.question: What is the difference between DevKitC and DevKitM?
faqs.answer: ESP32-DevKitC features the ESP32-WROOM-32 module with 38 GPIOs available. ESP32-DevKitM-1 uses the compact ESP32-MINI-1 module with 24 GPIOs. DevKitC is larger (55x29mm) while DevKitM is more compact (48x27mm). Both include the same USB-to-UART bridge, voltage regulator, and basic peripherals. DevKitC is better for projects requiring many GPIOs. DevKitM is ideal for prototyping space-constrained designs. Both support the same software development tools and frameworks.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use DevKitC for maximum GPIO availability. Use DevKitM for compact prototype designs.

faqs.question: Do I need additional hardware to start developing?
faqs.answer: Beyond the DevKit board, you'll need: USB cable (micro-USB or USB-C depending on board version). Computer with Windows, macOS, or Linux. Development environment (Arduino IDE, ESP-IDF, or PlatformIO). That's it for basic development! Optional additions include: Breadboard and jumper wires for circuit prototyping. Sensors and actuators for your specific application. Multimeter for debugging. Logic analyzer for advanced troubleshooting. BeiLuo offers starter kits with DevKit boards and essential accessories.
faqs.decisionGuide: Just a USB cable and computer are required. Add components as needed for your specific project.

products.name: ESP32 Development Board
products.shortDescription: Official ESP32 development board with ESP32-WROOM-32 module, USB programming, and breadboard-compatible headers for rapid prototyping.
descriptionParagraphs: The ESP32-DevKitC is Espressif's official development board featuring the ESP32-WROOM-32 module.
With onboard USB-to-UART bridge, voltage regulator, and all GPIOs broken out to headers, it provides everything needed for ESP32 development.
The board is compatible with Arduino IDE, ESP-IDF, and PlatformIO, making it the ideal starting point for ESP32 projects.

faeReview.author: Robert Taylor
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Developer Relations
faeReview.content: The ESP32-DevKitC is the board I recommend to every developer starting with ESP32. It's the reference design that just works - reliable, well-documented, and compatible with all development tools. I've used hundreds of these boards in workshops and customer projects over the years. The CP2102 USB bridge provides stable serial communication across Windows, Mac, and Linux. The GPIO labeling is clear, and the breadboard compatibility makes circuit prototyping easy. For production designs, you can use the DevKitC for initial development then migrate to your custom PCB with confidence. It's affordable enough to dedicate one to each project without breaking the budget.

alternativeParts.reason: Compact version for space-constrained prototype designs
alternativeParts.useCase: Prototyping designs that will use ESP32-MINI modules

alternativeParts.reason: RISC-V development with native USB and BLE 5.0
alternativeParts.useCase: ESP32-C3 evaluation and low-power BLE development

companionParts.description: Half-size breadboard for circuit prototyping
Male-to-male jumper wires for breadboard connections
Temperature and humidity sensor for IoT projects
Micro-USB cable for programming and power

faqs.question: How do I get started with ESP32-DevKitC?
faqs.answer: Getting started is easy: Connect DevKitC to your computer with USB cable. Install Arduino IDE and add ESP32 board support. Select 'ESP32 Dev Module' from Tools > Board menu. Choose the correct COM port. Open the Blink example and upload. The onboard LED should start blinking. From there, explore the ESP32 examples including Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and sensor reading. Espressif's Getting Started guide provides detailed instructions for Arduino and ESP-IDF setup.
faqs.decisionGuide: Follow Espressif's Getting Started guide for step-by-step Arduino or ESP-IDF setup.

faqs.question: What programming environments work with DevKitC?
faqs.answer: ESP32-DevKitC supports multiple development environments: Arduino IDE - Beginner-friendly with extensive libraries. ESP-IDF - Official Espressif framework with full hardware access. PlatformIO - Cross-platform IDE with advanced debugging. MicroPython - Python scripting for rapid development. ESPHome - YAML-based configuration for home automation. NodeMCU Lua - Lua scripting for IoT applications. All environments support the DevKitC without modifications. Choose based on your experience level and project requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Arduino for beginners. Use ESP-IDF for professional development. Use PlatformIO for advanced debugging.

faqs.question: How do I upload code to DevKitC?
faqs.answer: Uploading code to DevKitC: Connect USB cable to computer. Select correct board (ESP32 Dev Module) in IDE. Choose correct COM port. Click Upload button in IDE. Hold BOOT button if automatic upload fails. Release BOOT button when upload starts. Wait for 'Done uploading' message. Press EN button to reset and run new code. Most modern IDEs handle the BOOT button sequence automatically. If upload fails, manually hold BOOT while pressing EN to enter download mode.
faqs.decisionGuide: Modern IDEs auto-handle upload. Manual BOOT+EN sequence for troubleshooting.

faqs.question: Can I power DevKitC from external sources?
faqs.answer: Yes, DevKitC can be powered multiple ways: USB port (5V) - Primary power and programming. 5V pin - External 5V regulated supply. 3.3V pin - External 3.3V regulated supply (bypasses onboard regulator). VIN pin - 5-12V unregulated input (through onboard regulator). The onboard AMS1117 regulator converts 5V to 3.3V for the ESP32. Maximum current draw is approximately 500mA. For battery operation, connect 3.7V LiPo to 3.3V pin or use 5V boost converter to 5V pin.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use USB for development. Use 5V or VIN for external power. Use 3.3V for battery operation.

faqs.question: What is the pinout of ESP32-DevKitC?
faqs.answer: ESP32-DevKitC breaks out all 38 GPIOs with standard naming: Left side - VP, VN, D34, D35, D32, D33, D25, D26, D27, D14, D12, D13, D15, D2, D0, D4, D16, D17, D5, D18, D19, D21. Right side - 3.3V, EN, D23, D22, D1, D3, D21, D19, D18, D5, D17, D16, D4, D0, D2, D15, D13, D12, D14, D27, D26, D25, GND. All pins support digital I/O. Some pins have special functions at boot (D0, D2, D12, D15). Refer to the silkscreen labels and Espressif documentation for detailed pin functions.
faqs.decisionGuide: Refer to DevKitC schematic for detailed pin functions and strapping pin requirements.

faqs.question: Can I use DevKitC for production designs?
faqs.answer: DevKitC is designed for development and prototyping, not production. For production: Design your own PCB with the ESP32 module. Include only the peripherals your application needs. Optimize power supply for your use case. Add proper EMI/ESD protection. Consider certification requirements. The DevKitC schematic is open-source and serves as a reference design. Many of the circuit elements (USB bridge, regulator, reset circuit) can be adapted to your production design. BeiLuo can provide reference designs and design review services.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use DevKitC for development. Design custom PCB for production with reference to DevKitC schematic.

products.name: ESP32 Camera Development Board
products.shortDescription: ESP32 development board with integrated OV2640 camera, microSD slot, and Wi-Fi for AI camera and video streaming projects.
descriptionParagraphs: The ESP32-CAM is a popular development board combining ESP32-S module with OV2640 camera and microSD card slot.
With 520KB internal RAM and support for external PSRAM, it can capture and process images for AI and computer vision applications.
The compact design and low cost make it ideal for security cameras, QR code scanners, and IoT vision projects.

faeReview.author: Kevin Park
faeReview.title: FAE - Computer Vision
faeReview.content: The ESP32-CAM is the most cost-effective way to add vision capabilities to IoT projects. At under $10, you get an ESP32 with a 2MP camera that can stream video over Wi-Fi. I've used it for everything from security cameras to QR code readers to plant monitoring systems. The image quality from the OV2640 is surprisingly good for the price. With external PSRAM, you can buffer full VGA frames for processing. The ESP-WHO framework provides face detection examples that work well. For production, you'll want to design a custom PCB with better power management, but for prototyping and maker projects, ESP32-CAM is unbeatable value.
faeReview.highlight: Best value camera module for ESP32 projects

alternativeParts.reason: Full-featured dev kit with display and debugging
alternativeParts.useCase: Professional camera development with debugging capabilities

alternativeParts.reason: AI-enabled camera with voice wake-word support
alternativeParts.useCase: AI camera applications with face recognition and voice control

companionParts.description: USB-to-UART programmer for ESP32-CAM (no onboard USB)
Programming adapter with USB and reset circuit
VGA camera alternative for ESP32-CAM
Storage for images and video recording

faqs.question: How do I program ESP32-CAM?
faqs.answer: ESP32-CAM requires an external programmer since it lacks onboard USB: Use an FTDI USB-to-UART adapter or Arduino as ISP. Connect TX, RX, GND, and 5V between programmer and CAM. Connect GPIO0 to GND to enter download mode. Press reset button or power cycle to start programming. Remove GPIO0-GND connection after programming. Use ESP32-CAM-MB adapter for easier programming. Some USB-to-UART adapters provide automatic reset circuitry. Always use 5V power as the onboard regulator converts to 3.3V.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ESP32-CAM-MB adapter for easiest programming. Use FTDI adapter for flexibility.

faqs.question: What camera features does ESP32-CAM support?
faqs.answer: ESP32-CAM with OV2640 supports: Resolutions from 160x120 to 1600x1200 (UXGA). JPEG compression for efficient storage and transmission. Various special effects (sepia, negative, etc.). Exposure and white balance control. The ESP32 can process VGA streams at 15-30fps with JPEG encoding. For higher resolutions, frame rate decreases. The camera interface uses 8-bit parallel data with XCLK, PCLK, and sync signals. The ESP-WHO framework provides face detection running at 5-10fps on VGA streams.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use VGA for real-time processing. Use higher resolutions for still photography.

faqs.question: Can ESP32-CAM do face recognition?
faqs.answer: ESP32-CAM can perform face detection using the ESP-WHO framework. Face recognition (identifying specific people) requires more processing power and memory than basic detection. With external PSRAM, basic recognition of a few faces is possible. For reliable face recognition, ESP32-S3 with AI acceleration is recommended. ESP32-CAM is best suited for motion detection, presence sensing, and basic face detection applications. The ESP-WHO library provides pre-trained models for face detection that work well on ESP32.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ESP32-CAM for face detection. Use ESP32-S3 for face recognition applications.

faqs.question: How do I stream video from ESP32-CAM?
faqs.answer: ESP32-CAM can stream video over Wi-Fi using multiple protocols: HTTP/MJPEG streaming to web browsers. RTSP for IP camera applications. WebRTC for low-latency streaming. ESP32-CAM Arduino examples include a web server that streams MJPEG at 10-20fps VGA quality. For better performance, use lower resolutions (QVGA) or optimize JPEG quality settings. The limited RAM requires careful buffer management for streaming. External PSRAM helps significantly for higher quality streams.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use MJPEG over HTTP for web viewing. Use RTSP for IP camera integration.

faqs.question: What storage options does ESP32-CAM support?
faqs.answer: ESP32-CAM supports multiple storage options: microSD card slot for images and video (up to 4GB FAT32). SPIFFS or LittleFS in flash for small files. External PSRAM for temporary image buffers. microSD is the primary storage for captured images and recorded video. Use Class 10 or better cards for video recording. The SD card connects via SPI interface (SDIO mode not supported). For time-lapse applications, large microSD cards enable days or weeks of recording.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use microSD for image storage. Use PSRAM for processing buffers. Use flash for configuration.

faqs.question: Can I use ESP32-CAM in production products?
faqs.answer: ESP32-CAM is primarily for prototyping and education. For production camera products: Design custom PCB with proper power management. Add voltage regulators and protection circuits. Consider thermal management for continuous operation. Add proper mounting for the camera module. Include ESD protection on all interfaces. Design appropriate enclosure with proper camera window. The ESP32-CAM schematic is available as a reference. BeiLuo can assist with production design reviews and component sourcing for camera applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ESP32-CAM for prototyping. Design custom PCB for production camera products.
